title: Here’s How Long It Will Take Plant Life to Die Off Completely
gist: A Journal of Geophysical Research study finds Earth can support plant life for longer than previously estimated, governed by the sun's gradual brightening and the slow carbon-silicate cycle that regulates atmospheric CO2.

insights.claim: A new Journal of Geophysical Research: Atmospheres study estimates Earth will retain its ability to support plant life longer than previously thought.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Revises the timeline for the biosphere's ultimate end upward.

insights.claim: Billions of years before the sun becomes a red giant, it will grow about 20 percent brighter, heating Earth and altering atmospheric CO2 that plants depend on.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Identifies solar brightening, not red-giant expansion, as the near-term threat to plants.
insights.locator: At some point in the distant future

insights.claim: The carbon-silicate cycle regulates atmospheric CO2 over 500,000 to 1 million-year timescales by cycling carbon from volcanic venting through carbonate rocks, subduction, and back into the atmosphere.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Explains the geological thermostat governing long-run CO2 and habitability.
